item.title: Magnum Opus
item.description: Magnum Opus is an artistic and experimental project that merges ceramic research with alchemical symbolism. The work consists of a vertical cabinet divided into four layers, each representing one of the traditional stages of alchemy: Nigredo, Albedo, Citrinitas, and Rubedo. Whithin each level are displayed materials collected and developed during the research process: samples of raw minerals, high-temperature glaze tests, and ceramic objects. The material used include chrysocolla, aquamarine, diopside, obsidian, and lapis lazuli, all integrated into glaze recipes developed from natural raw materials.

item.title: Seven Stages of Enlightenment
item.description: Seven Stages of Enlightenment is a ceramic series composed of seven porcelain spheres, each finished with a distinct glaze formula developed through sustained material research. The glazes originated from an ongoing project focused on glaze chemistry, chromatic development, and the interaction between composition and firing atmosphere. Each sphere functions as both sculptural object and test site: a controlled yet responsive field in which variation in silica, fluxes, alumina content, and kiln conditions generate finishes ranging from vitreous gloss to matte, from fluid movement to crystalline structuring.

item.title: AlchemYst in Progress
item.description: AlchemYst in Progress is a living installation that situates alchemical symbolism within the concrete processes of botanical life. At its center stands a compact sphere of soil, enclosed within a transparent, spherical glass vase. This vessel operates both as container and lens: it isolates the soil mass from its sourroundings while making its internal processes visible. The sphere becomes a contemporary Prima Materia, dense, undifferentiated ground held in suspension, observed rather than dispersed.

item.title: Meditation
item.description:
Meditation is a mixed-media installation composed of three primary elements: a low wooden table positioned against a white wall, a transparent glass chalice containing raw honey, and a sand-filled cushion placed directly on the floor as a point of kneeling or seated engagement. The configuration is sparse and frontal, establishing a clear spatial hierarchy that directs attention toward the central object while simultaneously implicating the body of the viewer.

The materials are selected for both sensory and symbolic resonance. The glass chalice introduces transparency and fragility; it recalls liturgical vessels and laboratory glassware, situatinng the work between ritual and observation. The honey carries chromatic warmth and olfactory presence. Its slow movement and resistance to flow suggest concentration and refinement. The sand within the cushion provides softness and instability: it shifts under weight, recording the temporary imprint of each participant before gradually erasing it.

item.title: Enlightenment
item.description: Enligtenment is a large scale installation that merges astrophysical research, neuroscience, and spiritual symbolism into spatial experience. The project originated from the study of black hole physics, particularly the structure of the event horizon, accretion disk, singularity, and the bending of light thorugh gravitational lensing. The initial concept proposed a black metal sphere, cold and opaque, enclosing a radiant core of heat and light: a physical analogy to a black hole’s invisible interior.

item.title: ABOUT ALEX SALVI
item.description:
Alex Salvi is an Italian ceramic researcher and multidisciplinary artist whose practice centers on porcelain and high-temperature ceramic processes. Their work explores transformation, instability, and the interaction between controlled systems and unpredictable materials behaviors.

Working primarily with ceramics, Salvi combines sculptural practice with experimental research into glaze chemistry, minerals, and raw geological materials. Through slip casting, mold making, and experimental glaze development, they investigates how materials respond to extreme firing conditions and how small variations can generate unexpected surfaces, structures, and forms.
